title: Shinji Asuka and Rei Step Down for the New Characters
display_title: Shinji Asuka and Rei Step Down for the New Characters
wikitext:
[]: Shinji, Asuka and Rei Step Down for the New Characters             Quote Post by brankoburcksen » Mon Jan 02, 2012 5:02 pm The silhouettes on the Tokyo Evangelion Store feature never before seen characters (http://www.animenewsnetwork.com/interest/2011-11-23/tokyo-evangelion-store-displays-unidentified-characters) who presumably will be introduced in the forthcoming movies. Considering the Japanese naming scheme for the films with the third being called Kyu, which is supposed to bring the conflict to a speedy resolution, what then is the purpose of the fourth film? With the just released English title "Evangelion 3.0 You Can (Not) Redo" can it really be expected to be some sort of rehash of the events in the TV series and "The End of Evangelion" with similar but different plot and tone? And isn't the fourth film supposed to be a whole new ending? What then does entirely new really mean? I thought back on Shinji's final line in "Evangelion 2.0": "This is all I need." For the first time we see all three children pilot the EVAs for their own personal reasons rather than for some general purpose like because people tell them to, because they want to be praised or to save humanity. Asuka pilots EVA-03 so Rei can throw her dinner party. In the end, Rei pilots the EVA so Shinji doesn't have to, and he gets back in it to save her. Instead of retracting these gains made through the drama of the second movie, couldn't the third film finally put their issues about the EVAs and their personal conflicts to rest, so they can move forward to other concerns? As their major conflicts reach a climax, we are introduced to new characters besides Mari with their own problems and goals, which by the fourth movie would take center stage. Shinji, Asuka and Rei would still be there dealing with their own sides of the conflict, but they would be in more of a supporting role. I know it sounds sacrilegious, yet I believed one of the purposes of these films was to fight the stagnation in anime. Shinji, Rei and Asuka have been the pinnacle of these character types that have dominated anime for so long. To really breath fresh life into the industry, wouldn't one of the most ambitious approaches be to resolve the major inner conflicts of these characters while transitioning to new ones to set a new precedent for anime arch-types? It would be a truly new Evangelion paving the way for a whole new era. It wouldn't get rid of the old so much as setting it aside for the new to have a turn. To be honest, I'm not sure this would really happen, but for me, the purpose is to consider what "new" really means. Can there be a truly new Evangelion with the ambition to take us into the unknown, or is it all in the past? Any thoughts?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Jan 02, 2012 5:07 pm The preview for 2.0 showed Mari; there were no such surprises in the preview for Q; and the mini-trailer is all Space-Pirate Nekomimi Asuka all the time.

[]:             Quote Post by Ironfoot » Mon Jan 02, 2012 5:15 pm Too much of a dramatic change and loss of profit IMO; the trio remain some of the highest grossing figures in the industry, shoving them aside into support roles is a major gamble only a fool and his money would do. Rather, expect more pilots like Mari to come in. Supportive for the most part, active when needed to build character.

[]:             Quote Post by TheFriskyIan » Mon Jan 02, 2012 7:10 pm Ironfoot wrote: shoving them aside into support roles is a major gamble only a fool and his money would do.

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Mon Jan 02, 2012 7:20 pm In regards it's too much of change especially regarding a series of four fillms. After spending three films with the same characters. It would be difficult to move those three to side-characters. Then trying to get us to actually care about the new characters. Especially in the span of 2-3 hours.

[]:             Quote Post by Jornophelanthas » Mon Jan 02, 2012 7:47 pm TheFriskyIan wrote: Well this is Anno, we're talking about.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Mon Jan 02, 2012 8:11 pm ^ As if superficial fan and critical fan cannot be part of a same person, of which Anno is possibly one too.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Mon Jan 02, 2012 8:17 pm Jornophelanthas wrote: 1. Rebuild 2.0 showed an inordinate amount of Asuka fanservice. What's more, the most extreme instances were when Asuka was depicted at her most vulnerable. Like the see-through plugsuit during her final phone conversation with Misato. The entire sexy-underage-girl fanservice set-up directly contradicts the topic of the conversation, which was about Asuka sacrificing her (proto-)sexual infatuation with Shinji and finding friendship and a sense of belonging in return. Anno gave the fans their service, but protested it at the same time.

[]:             Quote Post by Jornophelanthas » Mon Jan 02, 2012 8:26 pm Monk Ed wrote: Honestly, I don't see what the protest was. One could just as easily construe that Asuka giving up on Shinji means she's "available" for the audience's fantasies. Isn't that the excuse given for why attractive female characters remain inexplicably single, so that the audience does not lose interest on account of viewing them as "taken"?

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Mon Jan 02, 2012 8:54 pm The Mari doing Ritsuko thing is intriguing. Ritsuko no longer has a past history in Rebuild and we never see her engaging in any secret relationship with Gendou, so her part can indeed be moved to someone else. I think this should be further discussed in the "3.0 speculation" thread. That said, I am not sure Anno subverts just to protest. It is likely that he enjoys all the superficial things as much as the so-called "superficial fans". And instead of "protest" it seems to me Anno is more likely sending knowing winks about he is doing the subversion. But then it is also fine if some want to read that as "protest" or "criticism" from Anno :-]

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Mon Jan 02, 2012 8:58 pm You do realize the song is the complete opposite. It's actually rather hopeful. "Let's be friends forever without end" and "Let's treasure The joy of trusting each other So Long, farewell for today Until the day we meet again," those lyrics stick out the most. The scenes quite sad. The song uplifting.

[]:             Quote Post by Nonoriri » Mon Jan 02, 2012 10:32 pm Jornophelanthas wrote: 2. The original series Neon Genesis Evangelion was pitched as a super-robots-fighting-alien-monsters-of-the-week-show. However, Anno loathed this genre , and instead made the core of the show character-driven, rather than action-driven.

[]:             Quote Post by brankoburcksen » Mon Jan 02, 2012 11:46 pm [I'll post this in the Evangelion 3.0 speculation as well.] For me, it all comes back to the question of what "new" in these movies means. If it means Mari destroying the Rei clones like Ritsuko did in the series, that's fine, but it's not really new; it's rehashing what was done before. The new stuff that I really enjoy are moments like Asuka talking openly with Misato for the first time or the kids taking a trip to the aquarium rather than just alterations of events in the previous work like Asuka piloting EVA-03 or Shinji maintaining control of a berserk EVA-01 to save Rei. That stuff is also good because it brings to light a new way of looking at old material, but wouldn't everyone want something more than just a variation of what came before? I think Anno puts more satire of fan service into Evangelion and in particular, these new movies than just Asuka in some inappropriate out fits. Through two films now, we watched Kawouru eagerly await meeting Shinji (again?), which brings up the other two forms of fan service: religious symbolism which serves no real purpose to the story other than aesthetic effect and lead to years of theorizing. You can now add the idea that these Rebuild films are a sequel to the original series as well, leading to a whole new plethora of speculation and theorizing. Did anyone notice that Rei wears the same school uniform that she does in the alternate reality sequence from episode 26 in the 3.0 preview with the three young Rei clones huddled behind her? Is that an indication that both the alternate reality from the TV series ending and the events of "The End of Evangelion" will converge in Rebuild? Much like the use of religious symbolism it is not the point. It could just be a simple nod to the old series, nothing more. It is a mental tease, just like Asuka's new plug suit is an visual tease. Anno is hitting fans on several fronts: poking fun at those who treat these characters like objects, the ones that try to fit the message into a theoretical framework of theology and those that are simply still too attached to the original work. The point is really to distract viewers from what Anno is really doing like the fan service in the scene where Asuka talks openly with Misato for the first time. Dramatically it is a very new kind of scene for Evangelion at the same time it ratchets up the fan service, so the distinctiveness is placated by what we have come to expect from the franchise. The variation, but not really new, function like the rehash of the elevator scene does the same thing: bring forth something new while still tying it to a little bit of the old. It softens the blow of new events. Yes, Anno is telling us to "Get a Life," yet what better way to do that than by shifting the focus to new characters? Look at it as him telling fans to stop fawning over a series from fifteen years ago. The old characters are going to resolve their issues and move on to something new, so we should do the same. You could say, not running away in this case means not living in the past, realizing life entails we are always changing, so every time we become too complacent with familiarity we are stepping out of reality into a fantasy while the rest of the world changes and moves ahead around us. In the hands of a very skilled filmmaker, which I believe Anno is, in just a few scenes ranging from a minute to five (time how long any given scene in the movies are) you can give a lot of information, introducing characters, events and tone. Look at the difference between "Batman Begins" and "The Dark Knight." In the first you have one character, Wayne, with a dramatic arc while in the second you have two more characters with dramatic arcs, Gordon and Dent. Though playing in the first movie, Gordon's presence did not amount to one with the same dramatic weight, much like Rei in the first Rebuild film. However, in the second you have dramatic arcs for Shinji, Asuka and Rei. What made adding Mari so difficult to the second Rebuild film was the fact that though very different from the series, most of the scenes were rehashes of ones in the original series, so of course it would be difficult to add a new character to events that are just deviations of old ones. However, with the forth coming movies, there is going to be a lot we have never seen before, so it becomes more feasible to add more characters and give them a proper introductions and developments as well as significance in a story that goes in a whole new direction. Referring back to Batman, it's possible they plan to do something similar with "The Dark Knight Rises" so it is not an entirely unfounded conception. http://herocomplex.latimes.com/2011/12/12/dark-knight-rises-christopher-nolan-opens-up-about-bane-choice/ Besides the feasibility of adding new characters, could you accept an Evangelion storyline where neither Shinji, Rei nor Asuka are the central characters, yet still have important roles to play?

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Tue Jan 03, 2012 12:21 am Branko. I won't bring up Mari or Asuka. My opinion on those is they were handled awfully. Thats just bringing up my opinion. It's not like I felt like bashing but, it leads to my next point. It's the characters we know. You can't expect them to fall by the wayside. Asuka, Rei, and Shinji are the main characters. They still have many issues they have to work out. Plus they are what make it Eva. As I already said they're are two films left. If we add new characters to the mix it's going to be difficult to grow attached to them. Look we still have no idea what the point of Mari is even yet. So we have two more films. An already a new character who isn't lauded as the next big thing. Fitting screentime for new characters would only serve as a detriment. If Rebuild was a television series I would have no problem.

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Tue Jan 03, 2012 3:52 am I thought the transparent plug-suit of Asuka served two purposes. 1) Fan-service, of course 2) Support the reason of the scene visually, namely that Asuka strips down her shell and makes herself vulnerable due to talking with Misato. The idea that Mari eats everything up is intriguing, but I still hope we can see something completely new from her instead of more rehashed things. Also, i couldn't take a mental breakdown from Mari seriously. I long thought that the main trio will be sound asleep (or in mindfuck mode) for 30 Minutes of Q to give Mari and Kaworu time to get an proper introduction. BUT the teaser trailer says something like "Shinji awakes with Kaworu besides him, also the world has changed LOLZ". So I don't think we might EVER lose Shinji as the main character. Maybe Asuka is gone for some time, only to return in all her glory... And Rei meeting another clone (thus herself) would be nicely disturbing... (the teaser showed a picture of many Reis) BUT until now we have not seen a Reiquarium in Rebuild, so perhaps Anno was creative there and gave Rei a new background?

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Tue Jan 03, 2012 5:49 am From the trailer to the official website to the recent Khara new year card, I am sure Asuka will be back in big time. This is for sure.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jan 03, 2012 6:30 am Those three characters on the left will hardly be main characters. Only one of them is of "stardom age", if you will, in that she's a teenager in a film marketed to teenagers. The other two could be Seele members for all we know. (We've never seen their faces in NME, so literally anything is possible there redesign-wise.) Or Anno could finally be placing either Jet Alone or its substitute in the new films, and the youngster pilots the rival technology. (Would seem like a rather late addition to the story about now, though...) Or they could be from some other Eva universe entierly, either pulled from an Alternate Universe scene yet to be seen in the new movies, or just from some other continuity entierly yet to be revealed, because who said everything at the Eva Store was strictly from the new movies? There are just way too many possibilities at the moment, but it seems to me that if Anno's gonna tease about Mari, who's yet to prove herself fully as a main chatacter, he would have leaked something more about these characters if their significance In the films was equal to or greater than Mari's, especially since he's already gone to the trouble of making an all new 3.0 preview.

[]:             Quote Post by Jornophelanthas » Tue Jan 03, 2012 8:41 am I second the suggestion to continue the discussion on Anno's creative vision here: [url]http://forum.evageeks.org/post/515428/Evangelion-30-Speculation/#515428[/url] I think I made my point about how this creative vision pertains to the central characters in Rebuild 3.0

[]: Evangelions, COMBINE! BD             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Tue Jan 03, 2012 11:13 pm Jornophelanthas wrote: Actu However, Anno loathed this genre,

[]: Re: Evangelions, COMBINE! BD             Quote Post by symbv » Tue Jan 03, 2012 11:20 pm Alaska Slim wrote: In particular, this "omitted" scene , while not suggesting a relationship, does seem to indicate feelings from Rits... And perhaps the closest to an in-universe reference of devient Gendo-Rei relationship we've yet gotten (not that I'm saying that's happening either, still, the scene creeped me right the f*ck out the first time I watched it).
